video_title: Volts ϟ 6.10.2026 – Colin McCormick + Doug Bryan  (Why Is NERC So Worried About Data Centers?)
gist: NERC issued a historic Level 3 reliability alert because gigawatt-scale data center loads trip offline en masse in ~20ms during minor grid faults (e.g. ~1.5 GW of Virginia data centers dropped simultaneously in summer 2024), threatening cascading blackouts. The fix is mostly software/UPS ride-through and engineering at the load level rather than building gigawatt-scale ancillary markets; mandatory FERC-enforced standards are likely ~2027-2028. The panelists argue compliance is a minor lift versus supply-chain bottlenecks, that wilder data center demand projections are overheated, and that gas's 'firmness' edge over batteries is largely a mirage.

themes.theme: Why data center loads break grid reliability assumptions

insights.claim: Data centers interface with the grid via active power electronics (UPS systems) that sense voltage/frequency and trip offline within roughly 20 milliseconds at the first sign of fluctuation, unlike conventional industrial loads with rotating machinery that ride through disturbances.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: The 'weird' fast-tripping behavior, not just scale, is the structural risk; investors should track UPS vendors and ride-through-capable hardware as a forced upgrade cycle.
insights.quote: That tripping offline happens in an incredibly fast, incredibly brief period of time — 20 milliseconds or something like that.

insights.claim: NERC's Level 3 alert is responding to gigawatt-plus-scale instantaneous load drops, not the 100 MW level that grids already handle, because clustered gigawatt data centers can compound to 2-3 GW dropping simultaneously beyond anything previously contemplated.
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: Defines the magnitude of the reliability problem (GW not MW) that drives all downstream regulation and capex; relevant to siting and grid-asset demand.
insights.quote: Let's call it a gigawatt at least. That's what NERC are responding to with the Level 3 alert — gigawatt plus scale drops off the grid.

insights.claim: Correlated simultaneous disconnection happens without any cloud coordination because co-located data centers all sense the same physical grid conditions and are programmed identically, so they synchronize their trip-offs purely through electricity physics.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Means the risk scales nonlinearly with geographic clustering; argues for dispersion policy and against data-center-alley concentration.
insights.quote: They're responding to the physics of electricity flow on the same physical grid.

insights.claim: AI training workloads create large GPU-driven load ramps up and down ('training bursts') whose ramp rates in some cases exceed the ramp-rate regulations that conventional generators are bound by.
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: Ramp-rate caps on loads are a coming constraint; flexible-compute scheduling and storage buffering become value-creating.

themes.theme: The 2024 Virginia incident and tolerance mismatch

insights.claim: In summer 2024 a minor transmission-line equipment failure in Virginia caused voltage dips so small humans wouldn't notice them, yet roughly 60 data centers (~1.5 GW) simultaneously dropped off the grid and switched to on-site generators.
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: Concrete proof the risk is realized not theoretical; the anchor event regulators and investors will keep referencing.
insights.quote: We're talking 60 data centers, I think it was around one and a half gigawatts, effectively simultaneously dropped off the grid in response to that single transmission fault.

insights.claim: The 2024 event forced PJM and Dominion to scale back power plant output to protect grid infrastructure and avoid cascading regional outages, because ~1.5 GW of inertia cannot be marshaled on a seconds timescale.
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: Shows grid operators are already at the edge of manual mitigation; reinforces that the solution must be at the load level.

insights.claim: The core reliability problem is a tolerance mismatch: generators are required to ride through voltage and frequency disturbances while large data center loads trip at the first sign of trouble, creating severe instantaneous generation-load imbalances that regulators must now align.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Aligning load ride-through to generator standards is the central regulatory thrust; the equipment and standards bodies that enable it are the investable edge.
insights.quote: The large data centers basically trip off at the first sign of trouble. The generators ride through and I've got a pretty severe instantaneous imbalance between generation and load.

insights.claim: A NERC survey found roughly 87% of transmission owners and distribution providers lack clear facility design, modeling, and performance criteria for large loads in their interconnection requirements.
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: Quantifies the regulatory gap and the scale of new interconnection-study and modeling work coming; demand for grid-modeling and advisory services.
insights.quote: 87% of transmission owners and distribution providers don't have clear facility design, modeling, and performance criteria for large loads in their interconnection requirements.

themes.theme: NERC's seven recommendations and the regulatory pipeline

insights.claim: NERC's seven essential actions bucket into communication/modeling (standardized data collection from computational loads, grid stability studies, commissioning, fault recording) and operational implementation (protection/ride-through gap fixes), applying to both new and existing computational loads.
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: Maps the full compliance workload; retroactive applicability to existing loads is broader than ERCOT's new-only proposal and a bigger burden.

insights.claim: NERC's commissioning recommendation pushes for full-facility load and no-load tests with all compute equipment in place, but because data centers are built in phases this likely forces grid operators to continuously re-commission as load is added.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Phased-build re-commissioning is a recurring operational drag and a fuzzy trigger ('what counts as a change'); a friction point for developers.

insights.claim: NERC deliberately did not mandate voltage ride-through for large loads, instead using gentle 'where possible' language because no central IEEE-type standard yet exists and the industry needs validated, qualified compliant equipment first.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Signals a phased path to mandates; watch IEEE/standards bodies and UPS qualification as the gating items before enforcement.
insights.quote: It's a lot more gentle — coordination with computational load customers where possible to maximize the ability to ride through.

insights.claim: Current NERC guidance is voluntary; grid entities must respond by August 2026, new reliability standards will then be drafted, and enforceable mandatory standards (via the NERC-to-FERC approval pipeline) realistically won't be in force until ~2027-2028.
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete timeline for when compliance capex and penalties bite; near-term build-out is largely unconstrained by these rules.
insights.quote: I'll put myself on the spot — but 2027, 2028 is probably a realistic time frame.

insights.claim: Only a scattered few grid regions have already implemented voltage ride-through requirements for large loads (Southwest Power Pool, Germany, FinGrid), and they use inconsistent ride-through and reconnect rules rather than a consolidated standard.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Fragmentation creates a standardization opportunity and regulatory arbitrage across regions for developers.

insights.claim: Germany's decade-ago '50.2 Hz problem'—where uniformly programmed rooftop solar inverters all tripped at 50.2 Hz creating an unintentional synchronized cascade risk—was solved cheaply by a software push that randomized each inverter's trip threshold.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Precedent that the data center trip problem is largely a software-randomization fix, not a costly hardware build—bearish on expensive ancillary buildouts.
insights.quote: There was a solution — to reprogram those inverters to have a little bit of randomization on the exact number, so they weren't all perfectly synchronized.

insights.claim: Many data center UPS units use 'event counters' (e.g. trip after 3-4 voltage events in a few seconds) governed by the conservative ITIC curve, and the easiest grid-citizen fix is a software change to remove or loosen that counter logic.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Identifies low-cost software remediation as the first lever; hardware/battery upgrades are the second, more capital-intensive step.

insights.claim: Building grid-forming inverters with synthetic inertia is the better long-term reliability solution than building more spinning mass, since more inverter-based resources are coming online anyway and the fix is largely software.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: Favors grid-forming inverter and battery vendors over synchronous condenser/gas inertia plays over the long run.

insights.claim: Building a gigawatt-scale, seconds-response ancillary services market to absorb data center trips is the wrong, unscalable and uneconomic direction; the problem will mostly be solved at the computational-load level via ride-through engineering.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Tempers the bull case for batteries-as-ancillary-revenue at GW scale for this specific use; value accrues to in-facility UPS/ride-through instead.
insights.quote: Trying to build a gigawatt scale ancillary services market to protect against this possible load trip issue is probably the wrong direction.

insights.claim: If GW-scale data centers must firm up power with batteries anyway, an exploding ancillary services market (growing in volume and price) could restore the value-stacking thesis for batteries that critics said was capped by quick ancillary-market saturation.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: A nuanced bull case for grid-scale storage economics if ancillary demand truly scales up with data centers.

insights.claim: The reconnection problem mirrors the trip problem: a GW of UPS-backed load coming back online simultaneously is as dangerous as it dropping, and there are no broadly accepted standards yet for smooth, ramp-rate-limited reconnection.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: An under-discussed second-order risk and a future standards/equipment requirement; max-ramp-rate UPS logic is a likely mandate.

themes.theme: Behind-the-meter, co-location, and the gas-vs-batteries debate

insights.claim: Hybrid behind-the-meter setups (on-site generation plus a grid tie) can actually worsen grid stability complexity even as they reduce grid-tie draw, because there are more active components to balance; only a fully islanded data center eliminates the problem, and essentially nobody will do that.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Behind-the-meter is not a clean regulatory escape hatch; NERC wants visibility into the full campus including co-located generation.

insights.claim: NERC distinguishes co-location (data center physically sited at a power plant but both still connected to transmission, relevant to FERC's PJM co-location tariff directive) from behind-the-meter (generation electrically tied directly to load, not flowing through transmission).
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: Clarifies the regulatory taxonomy investors must track; PJM co-location tariffs are a distinct live battleground.

insights.claim: In ERCOT, gas entries into the interconnection queue are up 150% while some renewables are withdrawing, as hyperscalers lunge for gas as a perceived firm-power and reliability solution.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: Concrete near-term bullish signal for gas turbine demand in Texas; a headwind to the renewables decarb narrative.
insights.quote: In ERCOT, the number of gas entries into the interconnection queue is up 150%.

insights.claim: Projected new gas production for data centers runs around 10-11 BCF/day, but delivering it requires gas transmission, pipeline and distribution infrastructure that largely doesn't exist, raising the prospect of 'zombie' or partially completed gas projects whose economics fail one-to-three years in.
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: Cautions against pricing in full gas buildout; pipeline/permitting bottlenecks and methane leakage are the swing factors.
insights.quote: 10 or 11 BCF a day is what you're seeing. That's a lot.

insights.claim: Gas's vaunted 'firmness' over batteries is largely a mirage and partly cultural ('masculine energy'); operationally both deliver firm electrons, and long-duration storage advances may let batteries provide the firm power hyperscalers seek.
insights.speaker: David Roberts
insights.so_what: Bullish long-duration storage thesis (Form Energy named); challenges the gas-procurement consensus as duration/cycling limits get solved.
insights.quote: It's hard not to see an element of masculine energy involved in preferring gas or nuclear for your firmness over batteries.

themes.theme: Demand projections, build-out pace, and the upside case

insights.claim: Carbon Direct's analysis holds that the wilder data center demand projections are overheated and a majority of data centers currently in the interconnection queue will probably not get built.
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: Direct fade of the most aggressive AI-power-demand forecasts; even a ~40% completion rate is historically enormous but well below headline numbers.
insights.quote: A majority of the data centers in the queue are probably not going to get built.

insights.claim: Contrary to the assumption that NERC's requirements will materially slow data center build-out, the incremental compliance effort is minuscule versus supply-chain bottlenecks like securing switchgear, transformers and bulk power at all sites.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Reframes the binding constraint as supply chain (switchgear/transformers/turbines), not reliability rules—where the real bottleneck investments and shortages lie.
insights.quote: I actually think the incremental effort here is pretty minuscule, especially compared to the other challenges that rapid data center buildout already faces.

insights.claim: Compliance only becomes a real headache if regulators mandate ride-through before a clear standard and broadly available compliant equipment exist; if compliant UPSs are on the market, the developer lift is minor.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: The key risk to watch is regulatory timing getting ahead of equipment supply—a specific failure mode for project timelines.

insights.claim: Forecasting data center load is best triangulated from three sources—hyperscaler capex announcements and SEC filings, GPU/chip shipment data, and the interconnection queue (which requires a significant haircut).
insights.speaker: Doug Bryan
insights.so_what: A reusable methodology for sizing AI power demand; the interconnection-queue haircut is the critical analytical discipline.

insights.claim: A correctly designed gigawatt-scale data center—stacking on-site generation, battery backup, controllable flexible load and fast power electronics—could function as a giant virtual power plant and become a net positive contributor to grid stability via frequency regulation.
insights.speaker: Colin McCormick
insights.so_what: Upside scenario: GW data centers as grid assets/VPPs, a future revenue stream and a reframing of the load-vs-grid relationship.
insights.quote: That's a decent recipe for being a grid asset.
